title: What is a goroutine
instruction:
Imagine a library with one librarian who can check out books and reshelve at the same time — while a patron is looking up a title, the librarian is already helping the next person. That's concurrency.

A goroutine is a lightweight task Go runs alongside your main code. Start one with the `go` keyword.

Run this — the search queries and patron names print interleaved because they run at the same time.
successMessage: Searches and patrons mixed together — two functions running simultaneously. The `go` keyword is all it took. Goroutines are incredibly cheap — you can run millions of them.

title: Channels — communicate between goroutines
instruction:
A channel is a pipe between goroutines. One goroutine sends a result, another receives it. Think of it as a book-drop slot between two library desks.

```
ch := make(chan string)   // create a channel
ch <- "The Go Programming Language"  // send a title into the channel
book := <-ch              // receive from the channel
```

Sending and receiving block until both sides are ready — a built-in synchronisation mechanism.

Run this. A goroutine looks up a book and sends the result back through the channel. Main receives and prints it.
successMessage: The goroutine looked up the ISBN and sent the result back through the channel. Main blocked on <-ch until the result arrived. No shared variables, no locks — just a channel.

title: Send and receive multiple values
instruction:
A channel can send as many search results as you need. The receiver collects them in order.

Start three goroutines, each finding a book by ISBN and sending its title back. Receive all three results and print them.

Add a third goroutine that looks up ISBN 978014 and receives its result.
hint: go findBook(978014, ch)  then result3 := <-ch  fmt.Println(result3)

codeChecks.message: Receive the third result from the channel.
Print the third book result containing ISBN 978014.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Three goroutines, three results, one channel. The results may come back in any order — goroutines run concurrently and the channel collects them as they finish.

title: WaitGroup — wait for goroutines to finish
instruction:
`sync.WaitGroup` lets you wait for a group of goroutines to complete. Imagine telling a group of library volunteers, "Don't close the library until everyone finishes reshelving."

```
var wg sync.WaitGroup
wg.Add(1)        // one volunteer to wait for
go func() {
    defer wg.Done()  // signal when done
    // ...
}()
wg.Wait()        // block until all Done() are called
```

Run this. Three volunteers each process a shelf section. `wg.Wait()` blocks until all three call `wg.Done()`.
successMessage: wg.Wait() blocked until all three goroutines called wg.Done(). The "All sections complete" line printed last — guaranteed. WaitGroup is the standard way to coordinate a group of goroutines.

title: Buffered channels
instruction:
A regular channel blocks until the receiver is ready. A buffered channel has a queue — you can drop off books without waiting until the shelf is empty.

`ch := make(chan string, 3)` — buffer of 3. You can return up to 3 books before anyone picks them up.

Run this. Three book titles are returned without a matching receiver because the buffer holds them. Then they are collected one by one.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	ch := make(chan string, 3)

	ch <- "The Catcher in the Rye"
	ch <- "1984"
	ch <- "To Kill a Mockingbird"

	fmt.Println(<-ch)
	fmt.Println(<-ch)
	fmt.Println(<-ch)
}
successMessage: No goroutines needed — the buffer held all three books. Buffered channels decouple senders from receivers. Use them when you know the maximum number of pending items ahead of time.

title: Range over a channel
instruction:
You can loop over a channel with `range` — it receives values until the channel is closed.

`close(ch)` signals that no more search results will be sent. The range loop exits automatically.

Run this. The catalog searcher sends 5 book titles and closes the channel. The range loop receives all of them.
successMessage: range on a channel is clean and safe. The loop exits the moment the channel closes. Always close a channel from the sender side — closing a channel you are receiving from is a bug.

title: select — handle multiple channels
instruction:
`select` is like a `switch` for channels. It waits for whichever channel is ready first — like a librarian checking both the return desk and the request desk simultaneously.

```
select {
case book := <-returnCh:
    fmt.Println("returned:", book)
case request := <-requestCh:
    fmt.Println("requested:", request)
}
```

If multiple channels are ready simultaneously, Go picks one at random.

Run this. Two goroutines search at different speeds. `select` handles whichever arrives first.
successMessage: Fiction arrived first, non-fiction second. select handles whichever channel is ready — no polling, no busy-waiting. This is how Go implements timeouts, cancellations, and fan-in patterns.

title: select with default — non-blocking
instruction:
Add a `default` case to `select` to make it non-blocking. If no channel is ready, the default runs immediately — like checking the hold shelf and moving on if it's empty.

```
select {
case book := <-holdCh:
    fmt.Println("Book on hold:", book)
default:
    fmt.Println("No holds waiting")
}
```

Run this. The hold shelf is empty at first — the default fires. Then a book is placed on hold and the channel case fires.
successMessage: Non-blocking channel reads — perfect for checking queues, implementing try-locks, or polling without sleeping. The default case turns select into a non-blocking peek.

title: Mutex — protect shared data
instruction:
When multiple patrons browse the same shelf simultaneously, two of them might try to grab the last copy of a book — a race condition.

`sync.Mutex` protects shared data with a lock:

```
var mu sync.Mutex
mu.Lock()
availableCopies--
mu.Unlock()
```

Or use `defer mu.Unlock()` right after `Lock()` to always unlock even if the function panics.

Run this. A library counter is checked out by 100 patrons safely.
successMessage: 100 patrons, 100 check-outs, exactly 100 every time. Without the mutex you would get random results between 1 and 100. The mutex ensures only one patron updates the counter at a time.

title: Done channel — signal cancellation
instruction:
A done channel signals goroutines to stop — like a "Library closing in 5 minutes" announcement.

`close(done)` broadcasts to everyone waiting on it — all goroutines reading from a closed channel receive the zero value immediately.

Run this. The shelving assistant goroutine checks `<-done` via select. When main closes the channel, the assistant exits cleanly.
successMessage: close(done) broadcast the stop signal to the shelver instantly. The empty struct{} channel uses zero memory — chan struct{} is the idiomatic Go signal channel. context.Context builds on this same idea.

title: Worker pool
instruction:
A worker pool runs a fixed number of goroutines to process jobs from a queue channel. Think of 3 librarians at a help desk — each handling one patron request at a time from a shared queue.

This limits resource usage — instead of spawning 10,000 goroutines, you run 3 workers that each process many jobs.

Run this. 3 librarians process 9 patron queries from the jobs channel. Add 3 more queries to the queue.
hint: for i := 10; i <= 12; i++ { jobs <- i }  (add before close(jobs))

codeChecks.message: Send query 10 to the jobs channel.
Send query 11 to the jobs channel.
Send query 12 to the jobs channel.
Replace the TODO comment with the three new jobs.

successMessage: 12 queries, 3 librarians, total 1²+2²+...+12² = 650. Worker pools are everywhere in Go — HTTP servers, background processors, batch pipelines. Fixed workers, unlimited jobs.

title: Race condition — spot and fix it
instruction:
A race condition happens when two goroutines access the same variable without synchronisation. The result is unpredictable — like two patrons trying to check out the last copy of a popular book at the same time.

The code below has a race condition on `count`. Run it a few times — the result varies.

Fix it by adding a `sync.Mutex` to protect `count`.
hint: Add var mu sync.Mutex above the loop. Inside the goroutine: mu.Lock(); count++; mu.Unlock() — or use defer.

codeChecks.message: Add a sync.Mutex to protect the count variable.

successMessage: Check-outs: 50, every single time. The mutex serialises access to count — only one goroutine at a time. Run `go test -race` on your projects to catch race conditions automatically.

title: Goroutines are cheap
instruction:
A goroutine starts with about 2KB of stack memory (growing as needed). You can run thousands without issue. Imagine 1,000 library patrons all browsing simultaneously — goroutines make that trivial.

This example launches 1,000 goroutines. Each increments a visit counter. In most languages this would be thousands of OS threads — each using megabytes. In Go it is lightweight and fast.

Run this.
successMessage: 1,000 goroutines, exact count, no locks needed (atomic operations). In real Go servers, tens of thousands of goroutines run concurrently — one per incoming request — without breaking a sweat.

title: Build a concurrent pipeline
instruction:
No starter code.

Build a three-stage concurrent book processing pipeline:

1. `findBooks(ids ...int) <-chan string` — sends formatted book titles ("Book N") into a channel, closes it when done
2. `categorize(in <-chan string) <-chan string` — receives book strings, prepends genre (e.g., "Fiction: Book N")
3. `head(in <-chan string, n int) <-chan string` — only passes through the first n titles, drops the rest

In main: find books for IDs 1–10, categorize them all as "Fiction", then display only the first 5.

Expected output (first 5 fictionalized book titles):
```
Fiction: Book 1
Fiction: Book 2
Fiction: Book 3
Fiction: Book 4
Fiction: Book 5
```
hint: Each stage: make a channel, start a goroutine that reads from in and writes to out, returns the out channel. The head stage should use a counter and close early.

codeChecks.message: Define a findBooks function that returns a channel.
Define a categorize function that categorizes items from a channel.
Define a head function that limits items from a channel.
Each stage should return a <-chan string (receive-only channel).
Close the output channel when your goroutine is done.
Use range to read from channels in each stage.

successMessage: A three-stage concurrent pipeline. Book IDs flow through stages like books on a conveyor belt — found, categorized, limited — each stage running concurrently. This is how Go handles data streaming at scale. You now know concurrency.
